expectations.text: Covers security, compatibility, code quality areas
expectations.evidence: Covers security (CSP guidance, CSV injection prevention) and code quality (error handling, shell command practices, accessibility attributes) well. However, there is no dedicated compatibility section -- no discussion of cross-browser compatibility, deprecated APIs, or platform compatibility. Findings touch on language tagging (WCAG) and hardcoded localhost, but these are not framed as compatibility concerns.

expectations.text: Lists BP-coded items or equivalent checklist
expectations.evidence: No BP-coded items. Uses numbered findings 1-8 with severity levels (Issue/Advisory). While structured as a checklist-like summary table, it does not follow BP-coded format or an equivalent standardized checklist.

Each finding has a severity classification (Issue or Advisory) and the summary table clearly lists all 8 findings with their severity. The verdict section provides an overall assessment. This serves as a pass/fail equivalent.

suggestions: Without the skill, the agent treated the Markdown files as quasi-web content and found interesting issues (contrast ratios, CSV injection, CSP), but missed the compatibility dimension entirely and did not use a structured checklist.
The findings about badge color contrast (#ffc107 failing WCAG AA) and CSV injection are genuinely useful observations not captured by the with_skill run.
eval_feedback.overall: Partial coverage. Interesting and actionable findings for security and code quality, but lacks compatibility coverage and structured BP-coded checklist. The skill clearly provides better format adherence.
